text: The birds cannot be certainly identified; some would alter ‘kite’ to ‘stork’, and ‘stork’ to ‘crane’.
vv. 10-12. The repetition of 6.13-15 may be intentional, but is more probably due to a copyist’s error; they are wanting here in some Greek manuscripts.
The last clause of this verse does not yield any natural sense, either in the Hebrew or in the Latin, and it seems likely that the text as we have it is defective.

‘Uncomplaining’ and ‘silence’ represent a Hebrew verb which normally has that sense, but is interpreted by some, here and in a few other passages, as meaning ‘to die’. ‘A draught to dull the senses’; literally, ‘waters of a root’; the potion so described in Hebrew is probably the ‘gall’ offered to our Lord at his Crucifixion (Mt. 27.34).
